local_names.romanized_local: Mo yu zhou (Cantonese: Mak yu juk)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee/rice porridge class; no specific cuttlefish porridge entry exists. Mapped to the closest parent class FOODON_03301710 (congee).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers rice porridge/congee as a category. No specific cuttlefish porridge sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy; this is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for cuttlefish porridge identified in the published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of cuttlefish porridge is approximately 300 g, as typically served at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Cuttlefish Porridge — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cuttlefish Porridge (F05100026). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy: cuttlefish, cooked + white rice, cooked) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice porridge / congee (white rice base)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cuttlefish porridge exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 78 is assigned based on the closest analogous food: white rice congee/porridge (GI ~78–81, mean 78), as reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The porridge form of white rice has a higher GI than plain cooked white rice due to gelatinisation of starch during prolonged boiling. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 51.6) / 100 = 40.2, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 17.2 × 3 = 51.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 191 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 573 mg sodium, representing ~25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and avoid adding preserved vegetables or salted condiments, keeping total daily sodium within the 2,300 mg guideline; a single 300 g bowl contributes approximately 573 mg sodium and is acceptable within a low-sodium meal plan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~78) white rice porridge base with 17.2 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 0.4 g dietary fibre/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 40, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, substitute white rice with brown rice or mixed-grain porridge to lower the glycemic index, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.2 g/100 g) and low total fat (0.9 g/100 g) are favourable; however, sodium at 191 mg/100 g (573 mg per 300 g serving) and a high glycemic load contribute to cardiovascular risk if consumed in large portions or frequently.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, cuttlefish porridge is acceptable in moderate portions (200–250 g) when prepared with minimal soy sauce and salt; the low saturated fat content is beneficial, but the high glycemic load and moderate sodium content warrant portion control and pairing with fibre-rich vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g), negligible omega-3 fatty acids from cuttlefish, and a high glycemic load from white rice porridge may contribute to glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute white rice with brown rice or oats to reduce glycaemic load-driven inflammation, add anti-inflammatory garnishes such as ginger in material quantities, and consider supplementing with omega-3-rich foods in the same meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cuttlefish porridge (墨鱼粥, Bubur sotong) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese rice porridge dish made by slow-simmering white rice in water or light stock until the grains break down into a smooth, thick congee, then adding sliced or shredded cuttlefish (sotong) and seasoning with soy sauce, sesame oil, salt, and ginger. The dish has a mild, savoury flavour with a soft, silky texture; the cuttlefish contributes a slightly chewy, tender bite and a subtle oceanic sweetness. It is a popular breakfast and comfort food at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly garnished with spring onions, fried shallots, white pepper, and julienned ginger. Nutritionally, cuttlefish porridge is a moderate-energy dish (83 kcal/100 g) with low fat (0.9 g/100 g), low protein (1.7 g/100 g), and a high carbohydrate content (17.2 g/100 g) dominated by gelatinised white rice starch. The porridge form of white rice has a high glycemic index (~78) due to starch gelatinisation during prolonged boiling, making it a high-GI food with a glycemic load of approximately 40 per standard 300 g bowl. Sodium is 191 mg/100 g, primarily from soy sauce and salt used in preparation. Dietary fibre is low at 0.4 g/100 g. Calcium is relatively notable at 39 mg/100 g. The dish is suitable for soft-food diets and is low in saturated fat, but its high glycemic load makes it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Portion control and substitution of white rice with brown rice or mixed grains are recommended for metabolic health.
common_user_queries: Is cuttlefish porridge good for diabetics?
How many calories in cuttlefish porridge?
What is the GI of cuttlefish porridge?
Is cuttlefish porridge high in sodium?
Can I eat cuttlefish porridge if I have high blood pressure?
Is cuttlefish porridge suitable for a soft diet?
What is the difference between cuttlefish porridge and squid porridge?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100026) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice congee/porridge (GI ~78), which is the closest defensible approved-source match. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cuttlefish and white rice. All other -1 fields reflect genuine data gaps in the HPB record and USDA sources for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.2 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked cuttlefish and cooked white rice in approximate proportions consistent with this dish; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific cuttlefish porridge preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100026) or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. 3. sugar_g is 0.0 g/100 g as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source — white rice porridge with cuttlefish contains no added or intrinsic sugars at detectable levels. 4. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on white rice congee/porridge from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for cuttlefish porridge exists in the approved GI literature. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (78 × 51.6) / 100 = 40.2, based on a 300 g standard serving containing 51.6 g carbohydrate. 6. Halal status is assessed as likely halal (cuttlefish is a permissible seafood under most Sunni jurisprudence; no pork or alcohol ingredients in standard preparation) but formal halal certification status of specific hawker preparations is not confirmed.
